findings.suggestion: Use a parameterized placeholder instead of string interpolation: db.QueryRow("SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE email = $1", email) ($1 for pgx/lib-pq, ? for MySQL/SQLite). Never build SQL with fmt.Sprintf from caller-supplied values.
findings.summary: Line 12 builds the query with fmt.Sprintf("SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE email = '%s'", email) and passes it to db.QueryRow(q) on line 13. The exported FindByEmail(db, email) takes email as an external lookup key, so an attacker-controlled value like ' OR '1'='1 bypasses the filter and UNION/stacked-query payloads enable data exfiltration or modification. The code's own comment (lines 10-11) confirms the interpolation is unparameterized. The implicit invariant that email is injection-safe is never enforced.
findings.title: SQL injection via fmt.Sprintf in FindByEmail

findings.suggestion: After parameterizing the query, add a unit test (e.g. against a sqlmock or in-memory DB) asserting the query uses a placeholder and that an adversarial email string is treated as a literal, then run `go build ./... && go test ./...` and attach the output.
findings.summary: FindByEmail has 0 callers (blast-radius) and no accompanying test; the pre-review procedure gate rerun_relevant_verification is still blocked with no fresh command/test evidence. The function was committed with a known injection defect and no proof it behaves correctly, so the next agent has no guard against re-introducing or missing the vulnerability.
findings.title: Incomplete handoff: no test or verification for FindByEmail

nodes.label: Prior commits, decisions, agents, and command evidence

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

Single new file store.FindByEmail contains a critical SQL injection: line 12 interpolates the caller-supplied email into the query string with fmt.Sprintf rather than using a parameterized placeholder, and line 13 executes it via db.QueryRow. Assumption-integrity check: the file's stated intent (line 1) and inline comment (lines 10-11) both accurately describe this as an injection bug, so the comment will NOT mislead the next agent — intent and code agree. The genuine defect is the unenforced invariant that `email` is trusted input. Function is currently unused (0 callers) but exported and clearly meant to accept an email lookup value, so severity stays critical. No runtime/DB verification was possible (no callers, no DB), so this is a definitive static finding rather than an executed repro; the rerun_relevant_verification gate remains open but the static proof is conclusive.

Single new file introduces an exported FindByEmail that constructs its SQL query with fmt.Sprintf string interpolation of the caller-supplied email (source.go:12-13). This is a critical SQL injection and also breaks legitimate lookups for emails containing apostrophes — a user-facing correctness/data-integrity failure. The code's own comment acknowledges the bug. Fix is a standard parameterized placeholder.

Single new file introducing FindByEmail with a critical, self-documented SQL injection: user input is interpolated into the query via fmt.Sprintf (line 12) rather than using a parameterized placeholder. This is a blocker regardless of the specialist axis. On the Agent Handoff axis, the change was landed with the vulnerability intact, no test, and no verification evidence — an incomplete handoff.
